title: Move.toml pas/ptb deps pinned to 'main' branch, not SHA (non-reproducible)
description: The pas and ptb git dependencies are pinned to the branch name `main` rather than an immutable commit SHA. Any push to MystenLabs/pas#main silently changes the surface the audited code compiles against, including public functions, required_approvals, clawback semantics, etc. The Move.lock currently pins the testnet env to b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc, which is the trust anchor used by this review — but a `move build` that re-resolves the lock (or a fresh checkout on another environment) will pick up new upstream code without any PR signal.
impact: The audited behaviour and the deployed behaviour can diverge without review, defeating the purpose of the PAS integration audit. The integration-boundary findings in this report are only valid against commit b64f0c58c.
recommendation: Change both `rev = "main"` entries in Move.toml to the same commit SHA already pinned in Move.lock (b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc). Add a CI check that rejects any manifest with rev equal to a branch name. Document a dep-bump process that requires a regression test run and a re-review of the integration-boundary.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: pas_supply::burn blocked when mint/redeem paused prevents emergency burn
description: burn asserts `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Under normal Hadron semantics this is consistent with regulated `supply::redeem`. However, pause is often triggered during an incident precisely when an emergency burn is most needed (e.g. to destroy funds that were seized from a sanctioned account). Coupling burn to the mint/redeem pause means the same flag that stops minting also disables the remedial action. This is the same class of concern as LEADS §4 but applies to the burn path, not just approve_transfer.
impact: During an incident pause, a compliance officer with RedeemPermission cannot destroy PAS balances even after they have been clawed back from a bad actor. Seize-to-treasury-then-burn becomes impossible.
recommendation: Either (a) split into separate `BurnPausePermission` / `SeizePausePermission` flags that are independent of the mint/redeem pause, or (b) explicitly scope the pause to mint-only in burn/seize and document the design. Current spec text (context §7.2) requires `burn` to check `not paused` but does not specify which pause.

title: pas_supply::seize gated by mint/redeem pause blocks compliance action
description: seize calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Seize is a compliance action used against de-whitelisted holders — it should continue to work even when ordinary mint/redeem is paused for operational reasons. Coupling it to the same pause flag creates a DoS against compliance during a pause. Note the ticket design intent (context §7.2) says 'seize can extract funds from de-whitelisted addresses' which implies seize is a control-plane operation distinct from mint/redeem throughput.
impact: Pausing mint/redeem also disables the most likely use of seize (recovering funds from a now-blocked holder).
recommendation: Drop the `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` precondition in seize, or gate it behind a separate `SeizePause` flag. Document why the chosen pause scope is correct.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer gated by mint/redeem pause also pauses transfers
description: approve_transfer aborts with EMintRedeemPaused when `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` is true. Operators might wish to pause minting while leaving secondary transfers alive, or vice-versa. By overloading one flag, the system forces a single coarse-grained pause. Also note the error name/message is `EMintRedeemPaused` — which is misleading for a transfer path.
impact: Cannot independently control the two throttles. Error surface is also confusing: a user seeing 'mint/redeem paused' during a transfer will struggle to diagnose.
recommendation: Either introduce a distinct `TransferPause` flag with its own permission, or at minimum rename the guard/error to `ETransfersPaused` and document that mint/redeem pause also pauses transfers.
evidence: assert!(!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(), EMintRedeemPaused);

title: pas_supply::burn does not verify T is a registered PAS coin
description: Unlike mint (which indirectly checks via `assert_can_receive<T>`, whose DOF borrow aborts if T is not PAS-registered), burn does not check `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` or read any PAS-specific DOF before calling `treasury.treasury_cap_mut<T>()`. If T is registered as a regulated coin (not a PAS coin) and the caller has RedeemPermission for T plus a Request<ClawbackFunds<Balance<T>>> from an upstream policy, the function would attempt to burn via the TreasuryCap stored under the regulated registration. Today PAS policy registration prevents forging such a request in practice, but the safety of burn should not depend on invariants held by external code.
impact: Defensive gap. If upstream policy or account invariants change, burn could be used as a bypass to burn a regulated (non-PAS) coin via the PAS clawback path.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinRequired);` as the first check in `burn` and `seize` (mirroring the guard added to `supply::mint`/`supply::redeem`). Cheap check, tightens the authorization boundary.

title: treasury whitelist helpers assume PAS-registered T; opaque abort for non-PAS
description: `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `assert_can_send`, `assert_can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` all call `dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>())` without first asserting `is_pas_coin<T>()`. If T is not a PAS coin, the DOF does not exist and the borrow aborts with the generic `dynamic_object_field` abort code, not the well-defined `ENotPasCoin` code defined nearby. This makes error diagnosis harder and is a mismatch with `policy_cap<T>` which does assert via `ENotPasCoin`.
impact: Callers hit opaque abort for what is a well-typed usage error. Combined with the note in LEADS §2, a non-PAS coin with a crafted Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> (possible because `auth::new_auth` only checks `is_coin_registered`, not `is_pas_coin`) aborts inside DOF rather than giving a clean error.
recommendation: Prepend `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` to each of the 9 whitelist functions, or factor to a private helper `borrow_whitelist_mut<T>` that asserts and returns the table ref.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset does not check is_pas_coin<T> before calling upstream policy
description: On a second call for the same T, the function proceeds into `pas::policy::new_for_currency` which asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` (upstream policy.move L51) and aborts inside the dep. Hadron's own Treasury would also abort in `add_pas_coin_caps` on `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`, but only after the upstream call already consumed work. An early `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ...)` would surface a clean Hadron-owned error. Also relevant: if the coin was already registered as a regulated coin (phase-1 admin::register_coin path), this function aborts inside `add_pas_coin_caps` with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` — the user-facing error does not distinguish 'regulated already' from 'PAS already'.
impact: Diagnosability: harder to tell whether registration failed at the Hadron layer or inside the PAS dep.
recommendation: Prepend `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ...)` or a more specific `ECoinAlreadyPas` in register_pas_asset. Separately, consider distinct error codes for 'already registered as regulated' vs 'already registered as PAS' to make operator triage deterministic.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset / update_transfer_template do not call version.check_is_valid()
description: Both functions accept `version: &Version` and pass it to `register_transfer_template`, which does not use it either (only the object::id(version) is read to embed in the PTB template). The version invariant is enforced transitively because the caller must have constructed a `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` via `auth::new_treasury_auth`, which calls `version.check_is_valid()`. This works today but is fragile: a future refactor that changes how Auth is created (or introduces a test-only helper) could silently skip the version check on the PAS admin path.
impact: Defense-in-depth gap; future refactors could regress version gating silently. No current exploit.
recommendation: Call `version.check_is_valid()` at the top of `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, mirroring the defensive style used in `treasury::create`. Cheap and makes the invariant local.

title: No unit tests for any new PAS module (pas_admin, pas_supply, pas_transfer)
description: There are no `#[test]` functions anywhere in `sources/pas_*.move`, and the repo has no `tests/` directory at all. Every critical path is exercised only via the e2e TypeScript script in scripts/src/examples/full-setup.ts. That script runs on a live testnet, is slow, and does not exercise failure paths (amount=0, non-whitelisted sender, double-register, paused mint, cross-coin Request/Policy). Phase-1 code may have its own audit, but the new PAS surface is the highest-risk addition and has zero in-repo coverage.
impact: Regressions on the PAS surface are invisible in CI. Integration-boundary assumptions (e.g. the shape of Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>> or required_approvals keys) cannot be detected if upstream changes.
recommendation: Add at minimum: (1) pas_admin_tests covering register_pas_asset happy path, double-register abort, whitelist add/remove/set_send/set_receive, update_transfer_template; (2) pas_supply_tests covering mint amount=0, mint to non-whitelisted, burn wrong-policy abort, seize while paused; (3) pas_transfer_tests covering approve_transfer non-whitelisted sender/recipient, amount=0, paused, and the happy path. Use the upstream PAS test helpers (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/tests/e2e.move for patterns).
evidence:
module hadron::pas_admin;

use hadron::auth::{Auth, TreasuryAuth};

title: No tests for pas_supply::mint (amount=0, non-PAS coin, non-whitelisted receiver)
description: The mint path has four abort modes (amount=0, mint/redeem paused, receiver not whitelisted, coin not registered as PAS) and a happy path (balance deposited, event emitted). None is covered by in-repo tests.
impact: Silent regression risk on primary mint path.
recommendation: Add `pas_supply_mint_tests.move` with at least one test per abort branch plus the happy path, using `treasury::create_for_testing` and a mocked Account. Verify the `PasMinted<T>` event is emitted with the right recipient and amount.

title: No tests for pas_transfer::approve_transfer whitelist enforcement
description: approve_transfer is the permissionless autoresolve entry — arguably the most-called PAS function in production. It has no tests. The upstream PAS tests exist but cannot guarantee the Hadron-specific whitelist semantics here.
impact: A regression in the whitelist assertion (e.g. checking sender twice instead of recipient) would ship undetected.
recommendation: Add tests covering: (a) sender not whitelisted (abort), (b) recipient not whitelisted (abort), (c) sender with can_send=false (abort), (d) recipient with can_receive=false (abort), (e) amount=0 (abort), (f) paused (abort), (g) happy path (event emitted with right sender/recipient/amount).

title: No tests for treasury whitelist table operations
description: The whitelist state machine (add/remove/set_send/set_receive) is net-new and stores `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }` per address. No unit tests exercise transitions such as remove-then-add, set_send on already-removed address (expects `ENotInWhitelist`), set_receive after set_send, double-add (expects `EAlreadyWhitelisted`).
impact: State-transition bugs ship undetected.
recommendation: Add a table-driven test that walks through each allowed transition and each forbidden one, asserting both state and the emitted event.

title: pas_admin::update_transfer_template takes &Treasury, not needed
description: The function takes `treasury: &Treasury` and uses it only to read `treasury_id()` for `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury(...)` and then passes it to `register_transfer_template<T>` which only reads `object::id(treasury)` to embed in the PTB. The treasury id is already stored in the TreasuryAuth proof, so passing the Treasury object mainly exists to get its ID for the template. Acceptable, but it is idiomatic to take the bare ID when that is all that is read.
impact: Minor verbosity; forces a read-locked Treasury input for a function that only needs its ID.
recommendation: Either keep `&Treasury` for future-proofing (and document the reason) or switch to `treasury_id: ID` derived from `auth.treasury_auth_treasury_id()`. The latter lets the function run without contending on the Treasury shared object.

title: WhitelistKey doc comment claims Table<address, bool>; actual type is Table<address, WhitelistEntry>
description: The doc-comment for `WhitelistKey` says `DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist`. The actual DOF value inserted in `treasury::add_pas_coin_caps` is `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` (line 257-261 of treasury.move). Stale comment that will mislead future readers.
impact: Documentation drift. Could mislead an integrator reading keys.move to learn the shape of the whitelist.
recommendation: Update the comment to `DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets).`
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: Spec drift: single WhitelistManagePermission replaces spec'd Add/Remove pair
description: Per the PAS Modules spec (context §7.2), `add_to_whitelist<T>` and `remove_from_whitelist<T>` require separate `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`. The code collapses them into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` which is used for add, remove, set_send, and set_receive. This is a deliberate simplification but diverges from the spec and reduces the granularity available when assigning compliance roles.
impact: Operators cannot issue a role that can add-but-not-remove from the whitelist. Not a security bug — just reduced expressiveness.
recommendation: Either (a) document the simplification in the module doc with an explicit note that collapses the spec'd permissions, or (b) split into two permissions to match the spec. If split, update scripts/src/constants.ts to expose both.
evidence: public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: deployPasTokenTemplate only declares 0x1 and 0x2 as dependencies; no 0xc (CoinRegistry)
description: The publish command passes only `0x1` (MoveStdlib) and `0x2` (Sui framework) as dependencies. The template calls `sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw` which lives in the Sui framework (so covered by 0x2), but `finalize_registration` reads the shared CoinRegistry at `0xc`. The Sui framework may or may not require `0xc` as a publish-time dep. The regulated `deployTokenTemplate` (in phase 1 — out of scope for findings but readable for reference) likewise doesn't include 0xc. Worth confirming once more with the compiler since the bytecode is hard-coded.
impact: If Sui's publish dependency graph requires 0xc when using coin_registry APIs, the publish will fail. If not, currently fine. LEADS §16 surfaces this too — needs to be confirmed on a testnet publish.
recommendation: Either add `normalizeSuiObjectId('0xc')` (and possibly `0xa` for AccumulatorRoot if used) to the dependencies array, or explicitly document (via a comment) that only 0x1/0x2 are needed because coin_registry is reachable transitively and 0xc is accessed at runtime. Run a publish against testnet to confirm before merge.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset has fragile OTW name derivation from symbol
description: The coin type is reconstructed as `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}` where `otw = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. This duplicates the transformation logic in `patchConstants.ts` line 62-63 and relies on them being identical. Any drift (e.g. adding underscore collapsing in one but not the other) yields a coin type string that does not match the on-chain OTW, causing registerPasAsset to fail with a misleading error (bad type argument).
impact: Integrator footgun: a symbol containing characters that get replaced differently causes silent misregistration.
recommendation: Extract the OTW/module name derivation into a single exported helper (e.g. `deriveOtwAndModule(symbol)`) used by both patchConstants and deployAndRegisterPasAsset. Add a unit test.

title: scripts rely on .includes() substring match to classify object types
description: Classifying created objects by `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` / `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<` / `::coin_registry::Currency<` is fragile. A well-crafted user-coin name that contains one of these substrings in its generic parameter (e.g. a nested generic like `OuterType<sui::coin::TreasuryCap<Inner>>`) would misclassify. For the Mysten-template bytecode this never happens, but if the template ever emits extra object types or the coin-registry API shape changes, this silently misidentifies objects. Combined with no runtime type validation (e.g. `.startsWith(...)` + exact match), this is a silent-failure risk.
impact: If substring match misfires, the wrong object id is fed to `registerPasAsset`, which then aborts with a deep Move error about type mismatch.
recommendation: Use a more structural check: parse the type string, require it to start with `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<` / `0x0000...0002::coin::TreasuryCap<` (normalize) and match the generic arg to the just-published package id + symbol-derived OTW. Or, add an assertion that exactly one of each expected type was found and throw otherwise.

title: pkg() returns empty string when deploy output is missing, no guard
description: `pkg()` falls back to `CONSTANTS.package.v1` which defaults to `''` if `deploy-output.json` is missing or the field is empty (line 53). Every `tx.add(...)` call in pas.ts uses `package: pkg()` — if pkg() returns '', the produced move call has a blank package id. Some SDKs error sensibly, others produce a cryptic Sui RPC error. There is no up-front check that deploy data was loaded.
impact: Poor developer experience; misleading errors when a user runs operations without having deployed first.
recommendation: Add an invariant helper: `export function assertDeployed(): string { const p = CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1; if (!p) throw new Error('Hadron not deployed — run `bun run deploy` first'); return p; }` and replace `pkg()` call sites with `assertDeployed()`.

title: mintPasTokens accepts `amount: number` — loss of precision above 2^53
description: `amount: number` is a JavaScript `number` (double-precision float). The Move type is `u64`, which has values up to 2^64-1. For amounts above 2^53 (~9 quadrillion base units), precision is lost silently. For a stablecoin with 6 decimals, 2^53 base units ≈ 9 billion tokens — likely above practical limits today, but a bond-type asset with different decimals could exceed this sooner. The codegen-generated `pasSupply.mint` likely uses `bcs.u64()` via the SDK, which serializes the (potentially-rounded) JS number. Similar issue in the PAS-client `sendBalance` call in full-setup.ts (`amount: 100_000` — OK for this amount, but type system does not prevent overflow).
impact: Silent mint of slightly-wrong amount for very large asks. More importantly, the external API invites callers to hit this bug.
recommendation: Change `amount: number` to `amount: bigint | number` (or just `bigint`) and explicitly `BigInt(...)` before passing to the Move call. Document the unit (base units, not decimals).

title: register_transfer_template embeds object::id(version) but version changes on migrate
description: The PTB template captures `ptb::object_by_id(object::id(version))` which is the Version object id (shared, not versioned). This is stable across `version.migrate` calls because `migrate` mutates the inner `version: u64` field, not the object id. So the template survives upgrades — good. However, if a future upgrade *replaces* the Version object (new UID), the template becomes stale and all autoresolve transfers abort at input-fetch. This is why `update_transfer_template` exists. Worth ensuring the upgrade-path documentation in version.move spells out that the Version UID must never be replaced.
impact: Upgrade footgun only if future devs replace Version's UID.
recommendation: Add a comment to version.move `migrate` noting: 'the Version object UID MUST NOT change across upgrades — `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` embeds the UID in the off-chain PAS template'. Alternatively, use `object_by_type<Version>()` so the template remains correct even if the UID is swapped.

title: register_transfer_template template argument order cross-check — matches signature
description: The PTB template registers the arguments to `pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` in the order: (1) `object_by_id(treasury)`, (2) `ext_input<PAS>("request")`, (3) `object_by_id(version)`. But the Move function signature in pas_transfer.move line 33-37 is `approve_transfer<T>(treasury: &Treasury, request: &mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>, version: &Version)` — i.e. treasury, request, version. So the order matches (treasury, request, version). Cross-check confirmed: the argument order is consistent. NO BUG — this finding is a refute of a suspected order-mismatch; filing as info so the consolidator can close the concern.
impact: None — order matches. Filed to document the cross-check outcome.
recommendation: No action. Keep a test that calls the template end-to-end to catch any future reorder.

title: approve_transfer validates wallet sender/recipient but PAS moves funds to recipient_account_id
description: Hadron validates `request.data().sender()` / `request.data().recipient()` which are the WALLET addresses per upstream send_funds.move L22/L24. But `send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` (upstream send_funds.move L69-70) pulls funds to `recipient_account_id.to_address()` — the PAS Account ID, NOT the wallet address. This is correct per upstream design (LEADS §§1, context §6.1): the wallet is whitelisted, funds go to the wallet's PAS account. But the mismatch is subtle and worth a module-level comment explaining the invariant. A malicious namespace.move upstream change that decouples account-id-from-wallet-address would silently break the whitelist guarantee.
impact: No current bug. Defensive concern if upstream derivation changes. Spec in context §6.1 flags this as the intended pattern.
recommendation: Add a comment block in pas_transfer.move noting: 'Sender/recipient here are WALLET addresses per pas::send_funds. The upstream `resolve_balance` sends funds to `recipient_account_id = derive(namespace, recipient)`. Whitelist enforcement against the wallet address is correct iff account-id derivation is a pure function of (namespace_id, wallet_address). See pas::account::internal_send_balance.' This documents the boundary for future reviewers.

title: add_to_whitelist always initializes can_send and can_receive to true
description: `add_to_whitelist<T>` inserts `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }` with no parameter for the caller to pick. If a compliance flow wants to whitelist an address for receive-only (e.g. a cold wallet that must not send out), the caller must first `add_to_whitelist` (granting both), then `set_whitelist_send(false)`. This is a two-tx flow that briefly puts the system into a state where the address has send-power it shouldn't. Better: take `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` as parameters (or add a `add_with_flags` variant).
impact: Operational race: between the two transactions, a malicious actor controlling the newly-whitelisted address could send before set_send arrives. In a multisig flow this race is harder but nonzero.
recommendation: Change the signature to `add_to_whitelist<T>(treasury, addr, can_send, can_receive)`. Wire through pas_admin::add_to_whitelist with two bools (default true/true for backward compat via a keyword or separate entry).

title: WhitelistAdded event does not emit can_send/can_receive defaults
description: `WhitelistAdded<T>` only contains `addr`. Since `add_to_whitelist` sets both flags to true unconditionally today, an off-chain indexer must assume the default. If `add_to_whitelist` is later extended (see R5-024) to take flag parameters, the event becomes insufficient without a schema change. Adding the two booleans now future-proofs the event and matches the pattern of `WhitelistSendUpdated` / `WhitelistReceiveUpdated`.
impact: Event schema drift risk; off-chain consumers must duplicate the on-chain default.
recommendation: Add `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` fields to `WhitelistAdded<T>` and emit current values from `add_to_whitelist`. Also consider emitting a combined `WhitelistFlagsUpdated<T>` event from add/remove for consistency.

title: Move.toml uses 2024.beta but token_template_pas uses 2024 (inconsistent)
description: Hadron's Move.toml declares `edition = "2024.beta"` while `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. `2024.beta` is deprecated in favor of `2024` (stable edition). The global user-CLAUDE-md prescribes `edition = "2024"`. The mismatch between the two packages is also ugly — both should be on the same stable edition.
impact: Risk of semantic drift between the two packages. `2024.beta` may be removed in a future toolchain release.
recommendation: Change root `Move.toml` edition from `2024.beta` to `2024`. Verify with `sui move build` that no beta-only features are used.
evidence: edition = "2024.beta"

title: No explicit namespace binding: PAS Namespace and Hadron Treasury can be mismatched
description: `register_pas_asset` takes `pas_namespace: &mut Namespace` as a parameter. Upstream PAS has exactly ONE global Namespace per deployment (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/namespace.move L24-27 'singleton'), so there is effectively only one to pass. However, nothing in Hadron asserts this — a malicious caller with a custom test namespace (created via `namespace::create_for_testing`) could attempt to register assets against a fake namespace, potentially separating accounts (which are derived from the fake namespace) from the real whitelist (which is keyed by wallet address). The subsequent `policy.share()` would publish a policy object derived from the fake namespace. This only matters in a test context today.
impact: Mostly theoretical because mainnet/testnet PAS has only one Namespace and it's not user-creatable. But there is no Hadron-side bond between 'the' PAS namespace and this Hadron deployment, so a future PAS feature that allows user-created namespaces would silently break the whitelist guarantee.
recommendation: Store the blessed PAS namespace ID inside the Hadron Treasury (or Namespace) at registration time, and assert match in register_pas_asset. Cheap and defensive against upstream changes.
evidence:
public fun register_pas_asset<T>(
    treasury: &mut Treasury,
    auth: &TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>,
    mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>,
    metadata_cap: MetadataCap<T>,
    pas_namespace: &mut Namespace,

title: pas_supply::burn does not verify policy belongs to T's PAS registration
description: `burn<T>` takes `policy: &Policy<Balance<T>>` from the caller. The type system enforces the generic match, but Hadron stores its OWN `PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` in the Treasury via `add_pas_coin_caps`. There is no assertion that the passed `policy` matches the Treasury's policy_cap (e.g. `policy_cap.policy_id == object::id(policy)`). In practice there is only one `Policy<Balance<T>>` per T because upstream `new_for_currency` asserts `!policy_exists<Balance<T>>()`. So today this is safe by external invariant. But the invariant is load-bearing and not asserted locally.
impact: If upstream PAS ever allows multiple policies for the same T (e.g. via a different namespace path), `burn` could resolve a request against a different policy than the Hadron-registered one.
recommendation: Add an assertion that the passed policy's cap-id matches the treasury-stored PolicyCap. PolicyCap is derived from the policy via `derived_object::claim(&mut policy.id, PolicyCapKey())` upstream, so the PolicyCap id is a deterministic function of the Policy id — you can check this via `object::id(policy)` and `object::id(treasury.policy_cap<T>())`. Apply the same check to `seize`.

title: pas_transfer::transfer_approval_permit exposes permit publicly at package level
description: `transfer_approval_permit<T>` is `public(package)` and simply returns `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()`. Per upstream docs (see pas::templates.move L30-40), this permit is the proof that the defining module authorizes setting a template for the given approval type. Since `TransferApproval<T>` is defined in this module (`pas_transfer`), the permit is only constructable here — upstream would reject any attempt from another module because `internal::permit<A>()` checks the defining module. The `public(package)` visibility correctly restricts callers to the Hadron package (only `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` uses it). OK.
impact: None — visibility is correct. Filed as info to document the cross-check passed.
recommendation: No action. Consider adding a comment noting that callers outside the Hadron package cannot use this helper because `internal::permit` is tied to the defining module of the phantom type.

title: add_pas_coin_caps missing sender/namespace binding in emitted event
description: `PasAssetRegistered<T>` is emitted with an empty body. It does not include the treasury_id, the registering admin's address, or the policy id. For a compliance-critical registration, indexers and auditors typically want the treasury_id and the actor for at least traceability. Compare with `TreasuryCreated` which includes treasury_id, label, and admin.
impact: Weak audit trail. Consumers must correlate the event via transaction sender, which only works for non-multisig senders and breaks for sponsored transactions.
recommendation: Extend `PasAssetRegistered<T>` to include `treasury_id: ID, registrar: address, policy_id: ID`. Emit from `add_pas_coin_caps` or (better) from `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` where all three are available.
evidence: public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}
